clean_story:
Steel bones. Concrete skin.

The term skyscraper comes from ships’ tallest sails—"sky-scraping"—but these buildings outlast masts by centuries. A skyscraper’s life begins with its skeleton: steel beams bolted like a 3D erector set, then wrapped in concrete mixed to harden for 100+ years. The Empire State Building, finished in 1931, still stands because its 60,000 tons of steel were overbuilt—like a bridge designed for trucks carrying elephants.

Yet lifespan isn’t just strength. The John Hancock Center in Chicago (1969) got a $150 million facelift in the 1990s when its glass panels started popping out in high winds. Maintenance is constant: elevators replaced every 25 years, plumbing every 50, façades inspected annually via cranes or drones. Demolition is rare—only ~1% of skyscrapers are razed before age 50. When they are, it’s often economics, not decay: New York’s Singer Building (1908) was torn down in 1968 for a taller tower. The average lifespan? 70–100 years, but icons like the Chrysler Building (1930) may last 300 with care.

Three related subjects are urban decay patterns, how earthquakes test buildings, and why glass towers melt cars.
